Today is going to be a wrap-up. So this post mainly summarizes by way of retrospective.

I've actually been thinking about "what can Node.js do?" since the year before last, but never wrote about it. By the time I wanted to write, things kept getting in the way, so I never did. Then this year I finally got to it, only to run into all sorts of stuff. The original content was less padded than I wanted, and as the contest went on I got busier and busier — really hard to find time to write. So I just had to accept that, Orz.

As I mentioned, my topic this time is more about "what can Node.js do" — so you'll notice this series isn't focused on a specific Node.js + Web area like the very common Node.js + Express.js + MongoDB. Instead I directly challenged integrating various random things, like…
As I wrote, I noticed some knowledge points were really important, so I added more, like:

Toward the end, I also wrote about Google Extension and Google Apps Script. These two also use things related to Node.js. With Google Extension as the example, it's essentially a web page running in the browser — also relying on the browser's V8 engine. Google Apps Script's underlying layer also uses the V8 engine. So these two are kind of similar.
(Saying it like this, the topic might better be called "What can V8 do?" XD?)

The whole series doesn't have much continuity, but I still hope I've shown you that Node.js can do many things — not just web. There are many other fields too. Have to admit, JavaScript really is taking over the world.

For the very end of this post — although the content didn't quite hit the mark I'd hoped, I still hope I gave you a little spark of an idea. If you happen to be building a project, maybe you can find inspiration here.
See you next time when fate allows.
